problem_summary: 2015 MCM Problem B (discrete): design a generic mathematical model to plan the search for a lost plane believed to have crashed in open water, accounting for plane type, ocean drift, and allocation of search aircraft. This paper frames it as a recursive Bayesian search-theory problem, builds prior probability density functions (PDFs) over the impact location, updates them with (simulated) search results, and allocates heterogeneous search planes to high-probability cells. It validates against the real AF447 wreckage location and forecasts a region for MH370.
models_used: Recursive Bayesian search model (the core): grid the search area into discrete cells S_{i,j}, build a prior PDF over impact/target location, update to a posterior via Bayes' rule after each search phase, then recurse using the posterior as the next prior until the wreckage is found

Bayes' rule posterior update with detection feedback: P_D(S_{i,j}|B) = P(B|S_{i,j})P_D(S_{i,j}) / sum over cells, where B is the 'detected/not-detected' report and the search-aircraft detection probability P_{A_k D} encodes imperfect sensors (false negatives allowed, no false positives)

Collision model for drifter count: N_0 = alpha*V (max drifters proportional to plane volume), N = beta*N_0; two crash regimes — 'Air Disintegration' (constant beta) and 'Water Disintegration' where beta is driven by vertical impulse I = m*v*sin(theta) via a piecewise (0 / linear / 1) saturating function of I

Flight-dynamics prior PDF: a 50/50 mixture of (a) a uniform disk from the Last Known Position (LKP) bounded by max feasible travel distance to the missed-ACARS time, and (b) an empirical cumulative distance distribution fit from 9 past loss-of-control accidents, fitted as F(D) = 1 - 22/(D^2 + 22)

Reverse-drift prior PDF: back-drift spotted debris to the crash moment using OSCAR ocean-surface-current data with linear interpolation; wind/leeway folded in by setting drifter speed = 0.25 * current speed

Mixture-weighting scheme to combine priors: surface-search prior = 70% flight-dynamics + 30% reverse-drift; underwater-search prior = 70% flight-dynamics + 30% surface-search posterior (lower weight to drift due to ocean-geography uncertainty)

Monte-Carlo 'system/simulation module': computer-generated random motion + currents to synthesize ground-truth debris trajectories, serving as the oracle that the search model is tested and corrected against

Allocation model: Quicksort to rank cells by PDF (descending) and planes by capability (descending), then branch-and-bound to maximize cumulative detection probability sum P_{A_k W}(S_{i,j}) as the objective when assigning heterogeneous planes (helicopters vs fixed-wing) to cells
key_techniques: Discretize the continuous ocean into equal cells and treat target location as a random variable with a PDF — the canonical move that turns a search problem into tractable discrete Bayesian inference

Build priors by mixture-weighting multiple independent information sources (flight dynamics, empirical accident statistics, reverse drift) rather than committing to one, with weights reflecting each source's trust

Borrow the actual published methodology: the prior structure, the 50/50 and 70/30 weights, and the recursive surface-then-underwater pipeline are taken from the METRON/Stone et al. AF447 search-analysis report [3], grounding the model in real SAR practice
Empirical curve-fitting of a closed-form CDF F(D)=1-22/(D^2+22) to scattered historical accident data to get a usable distance distribution
Reduce a hard physics question (how many drifters?) to a small parametric model with a saturating piecewise impulse-to-disintegration map, keeping it analytically clean
Separate concerns cleanly: area-optimization (Bayesian search model) is decoupled from resource-allocation (Quicksort + branch-and-bound), so each sub-problem stays simple

Validate on a case with known ground truth (AF447, wreckage found 6.5 NM from LKP at 3900 m) to demonstrate the model finds it in ONE underwater pass vs the real four phases — a concrete, checkable win

Multi-axis sensitivity analysis (Tables 3-9) using a single scalar robustness metric, cumulative detection probability sum P_{A_k W}, swept over drifter count, plane count, plane type, and all three mixture weights to argue robustness
why_it_won: "It married a textbook-correct, literature-grounded method (recursive Bayesian search theory, the same family used in the actual AF447 recovery) to the MCM problem and then proved it on real data: the model's single-pass predicted region contains the true AF447 impact point, beating the real four-phase search. Judges reward a model that (1) is the RIGHT tool for the problem, (2) is decomposed into clean, individually-justified pieces (collision -> drift -> Bayesian update -> allocation), (3) is validated against ground truth, and (4) is stress-tested for robustness — and this paper does all four. The sensitivity tables showing the answer is nearly invariant to the prior mixture weights directly defend against the obvious 'your weights are arbitrary' critique, which is exactly the kind of self-aware rigor judges look for. The branch-and-bound allocation handles the part of the prompt many teams ignore (matching diverse search planes to areas), giving full-scope coverage."
section_highlights: "Summary clearly enumerates the model chain (collision -> simulation -> Bayesian surface/underwater search -> branch-and-bound allocation) and states results up front. Section 3 'Base Analysis' justifies why plane type and search-plane type matter (Table 1 compares SH-60B/P-3C/C-130H on speed, range, sensors), motivating the heterogeneity the allocation model later exploits. The Figure 2 flowchart of the PDF computation pipeline (flight-dynamics prior + reverse-drift prior -> surface prior -> surface posterior -> underwater prior -> recursion) is the structural backbone and makes the whole method legible at a glance. Section 8 is the strongest: AF447 validation with the true impact point overlaid on the predicted PDF (Figure 11), then an MH370 forecast (35S-39S region) that honestly handles the harder, LKP-unknown case. Section 9's six sensitivity sub-tests with the unified sum P_{A_k W} metric. Section 11's non-technical press-conference letter is empathetic, plain-language, and audience-appropriate (addresses grieving families, explains the plan without jargon) — a deliverable many teams botch."
pitfalls_or_limitations: The posterior-update demos are largely run on SIMULATED detection feedback (the 'system module' is also the oracle), so the Bayesian updates are partly self-consistent rather than validated against independent search data — the AF447 'win' actually relies on the prior PDF, not a real posterior recursion

Several physics simplifications are crude and acknowledged: drifter count N_0 = alpha*V ignores materials/buoyancy; all debris treated identically (bodies vs metal have very different leeway, which the paper itself flags); horizontal impulse omitted at impact
Leeway/wind is hand-waved as 'drifter speed = 0.25 * current speed' with the 0.25 chosen by intuition, not derived or calibrated — a weak link given drift dominates surface-search uncertainty
The mixture weights (50/50, 70/30) are imported from the AF447 report rather than independently justified; the sensitivity analysis defends robustness but the values remain borrowed constants
Several unknown parameters (alpha, beta saturation I_0 and gamma, detection probabilities) are 'obtained from practical data' in principle but never actually fit in the paper — they're assumed

Branch-and-bound is invoked but its formulation is thin: the objective (sum of detection probabilities) and the discretized assignment are described, but no explicit bounding function or complexity handling is shown, and the paper admits computation blows up with finer cells or more planes

MH370 section leans heavily on citing others' flight-path reconstructions (Figure 13, refs [18][19]) rather than the team's own model — the team's contribution there is mostly re-feeding those into the Bayesian pipeline
Minor sloppiness: figure cross-references point to 'Figure 1' where 'Figure 2' (the flowchart) is meant; some heatmap data tables appear truncated/garbled
reusable_patterns: Search/localization problems -> grid + recursive Bayesian update: discretize space into cells, put a prior PDF on the unknown, update with P(target|observation) after each action, recurse posterior-as-next-prior. Reusable for any 'find the hidden thing' prompt (search, surveillance, source localization)

Mixture-of-priors with trust-weighted blending when you have several imperfect information sources — and then RUN A SENSITIVITY SWEEP on the mixture weights to convert an arbitrary choice into a defended robustness result

Validate-on-known-ground-truth as the centerpiece argument: pick a historical case where the true answer is known (AF447) and show your model recovers it, ideally outperforming what really happened — instantly credible to judges
Decouple optimization from allocation: solve 'where is it most likely' (Bayesian) separately from 'who searches where' (sort + branch-and-bound). Two simple solvers beat one tangled one
Reduce a messy physical quantity to a small saturating piecewise function (0 / linear / 1) of a single driving variable (here impulse) when you need a plausible-but-tractable submodel
Use a Monte-Carlo simulation module as a synthetic ground-truth oracle to test/calibrate the analytical model when real data is scarce
A single scalar success metric (cumulative detection probability) makes multi-factor sensitivity analysis clean and comparable across all swept parameters
Treat the required non-technical/communication deliverable as a first-class section: write it in the stakeholder's voice (here, an airline addressing victims' families), plain-language, no equations
